content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
December 5, 1997
Lori Barker
Assistant City Attorney
City of Chico
411 Main Street
Post Office Box 3420
Chico, California 95927
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-478
Dear Ms. Barker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofPlanning Commissioner
Barry Belmonte regarding the provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1) May Mr. Belmonte, an appointee, contribute to a political action committee ifthe
person who appointed him solicits funds for the committee?
2) Does the definition of “person” as used in section 85705 include the city council as a
whole, or each individual councihnember, or both?
CONCLUSIONS
1) Yes. Mr. Belmonte may contribute to the committee as long as the committee is not
controlled by the person who appointed him.
2) The definition of “person” includes both the city council and each individual
councilmember.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, ofthe California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-97-478
Page 2
FACTS
The Chico City Council has seven members. All seven city councilinembers are elected
at large and the mayor is then selected by a vote of the councilmembers. Members of the Chico
Planning Commission are appointed by a vote of the entire city council, including the mayor.
Each member ofthe planning commission is selected and nominated by an individual member of
the city council, but a majority vote ofthe entire city council is necessary to ratifr the
appointment. Mr. Belmonte is currently a planning commissioner. He was appointed in 1995.
The current mayor of Chico, Rick Keene, was a councilmember at the time Mr. Belmonte was
appointed to the planning commission and voted in favor ofthat appointment.
The mayor has recently established what is referred to as the “Mayor’s Breakfast Club.”
An invitation from the mayor is mailed to potential participants to raise money for committees
that can help get the group’s message out to the electorate in the upcoming election.
-
Additionally, the mailer from the mayor states that he needs help raising money to retain the
conservative majority on the city council. Attendance at club meetings is free for members.
Membership to the club costs $250 per election cycle. Attendance for non-members at a club
meeting is $60.00 per person. The mayor is the speaker at the club meetings. Club contributions
are made to a committee entitled “Non-Partisan Voter Education Committee PAC” (NPVEC).
The NPVEC is controlled by a board of directors. The mayor is not a member of the
board nor does his name appear on the letterhead ofthe NPVEC. In addition, the mayor is not
involved in the development and implementation of strategy for the NPVEC. Neither the mayor
nor the mayor’s campaign committee coordinate or exchange speakers at events other than the
club meetings. The mayor and the NPVEC have not held, nor do they plan to hold, media events
together. The mayor does not use materials prepared by the NPVEC for distribution in his own
mailers or handouts. The mayor does not allow the NPVEC to make public statements that the
mayor supports the NPVEC.
The committee has filed FPPC Form 410 with the Secretary of State. The form identifies
the committee as a general purpose county committee. It does not state that it is a controlled
committee. The form and invitation from the mayor do not directly purport to solicit funds for
use in supporting the mayor in a future election, however, this possibility is not precluded.
ANALYSIS
Proposition 208 created a new prohibition on contributions from governmental
appointees to the officeholders who appointed them. Section 85705 provides as follows:
“No person appointed to a public board or commission or as
Trustee of the California State University or Regent of the
University of California during tenure in office shall donate to, or
solicit or accept any campaign contribution for, any committee
controlled by the person who made the appointment to that office


File No. A-97-478
Page 3
or any other entity with the intent that the recipient of the donation
be any committee controlled by such persons who made the
appointment.”
“Person Who Made the Appointment”
Mr. Belmonte is a member ofthe Chico Planning Commission. Each member ofthe
planning commission is selected and nominated by an individual member of the Chico City
Council, but a majority vote ofthe entire city council is necessary to ratif’ the appointment. The
Act’s definition ofperson expressly includes a “group ofpersons acting in concert.” (Section
82047.) In addition, each individual member of such a group is also a “person” and, as a result,
each and every member of an appointing body is also a person who made the appointment.
(Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-97-203.) We have advised that when an appointee is nominated by
an individual member of a city council and the entire city council approves the nomination, the
“person who made the appointment” is the entire city council. (Vasquez-Connolly Advice Letter,
No. A-97-181.) Thus, the person who appointed Mr. Belinonte is the entire city council and each
individual member of the city council at the time Mr. Belmonte was appointed.
Chico’s current mayor, Rick Keene, was a city councilmember at the time the city council
appointed Mr. Belmonte to the planning commission. Accordingly, Mr. Belmonte may not
donate to, solicit or accept any campaign contribution for the mayor or any committee controlled
by the mayor.
“Mayor’s Breakfast Club”
The mayor has invited Mr. Belmonte to join the Mayor’s Breakfast Club. The cost of the
membership is $250. Nonmembers may attend club meetings for $60 per meeting. The
membership and meeting fees are contributed to a political action committee, the NPVEC.
While the mayor is involved in soliciting funding for the NPVEC, the committee is registered as
a general purpose committee that is not candidate controlled. Section 82016 defines “controlled
committee” and provides as follows:
“Controlled committee” means a committee which is controlled
directly or indirectly by a candidate or state measure proponent or
which acts jointly with a candidate, controlled committee or state
measure proponent in connection with the making of expenditures.
A candidate or state measure proponent controls a committee ifhe,
his agent or any committee he controls has a significant influence
on the action or decisions ofthe committee.”
The Commission has interpreted the definition of “controlled committee” very broadly to
include any significant participation in the actions of a committee by a candidate, his or her
agent, or representatives of any other committee he or she controls. To determine whether a
candidate controls a committee, we look at the degree ofthe candidate’s involvement in the
committee’s activities.


File No. A-97-478
Page 4
We have advised that certain activities are not sufficient by themselves to constitute
control. For example, a candidate does not control a committee if that candidate’s only activity
with regard to the committee is soliciting funds for the committee. Other illustrations of
activities which are not sufficient by themselves to constitute control of the committee include
attending fundraising activities of the committee, or accepting campaign contributions from the
committee. (Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-197, copy enclosed.) However, such activities
may be relevant in determining whether the committee is, in fact, controlled by the candidate.
(WoodruffAdvice Letter, No. 1-89-180; copy enclosed.)
Mayor Keene is actively involved in soliciting funds for the NPVEC. This activity, by
itself, is not sufficient to establish control. Based on the facts provided, it appears that Mayor
Keene does not control the NPVEC at this time. Accordingly, Mr. Belmonte may join the club
or attend the club meetings without violating section 85705.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steve G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Alisa Fong
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:AF:jlw

councilmember.Page 2

person who appointed him solicits funds for the committee?

whole, or each individual councihnember, or both?

CONCLUSION: 1) Yes. Mr. Belmonte may contribute to the committee as long as the committee is not

2) The definition of “person” includes both the city council and each individual
